input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: ModelBased Systems Engineering (MBSE) is applied across multiple abstraction levels—from requirements to product—using SysML for structural modelling, particularly I‑Block Diagrams for component and connector relationships. Behavioural aspects are represented with simulation tools such as MathWorks Simulink, and general-purpose code in languages like C++ or Java can be integrated as needed. Architecture description languages (ADLs) including ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs are cited as methods for expressing component–connector architectures. Communication between the physical artefact and its digital counterpart is handled via MQTT, which provides an automated one‑way data flow for the Digital Shadow. No specific databases, version‑control systems, or cloud platforms are identified in the excerpt.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Digital Twin employs a multi‑level Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) model set, comprising requirements‑level specifications, functional architecture described with architecture description languages such as MontiArc (Automaton) and SysML state machines, logical architecture expressed via SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs) and ADLs (ACME, AADL), and product‑layer models realized in C++/Java code or Simulink. Data components consist of telemetry streams obtained from the physical system (unspecified transport) and are mapped to the corresponding model instances for real‑time monitoring. The models are linked via hierarchical composition, with behavioral models (Simulink) instantiated inside logical models (SysML IBDs) to provide traceability from requirement to behavior.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data comprising input current and output angle is continuously streamed from the physical asset to the digital twin as a time‑series via MQTT. Each message includes the sensor reading and a timestamp. The data flows are one‑way, with lightweight preprocessing performed on arrival to align timestamps and standardize numeric types before being aggregated into the virtual model.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: Development of a Digital Twin is carried out through Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) using SysML and other modeling notations. The process is divided into nested abstraction levels—requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers—each level building on the previous one. Transition between phases is managed by cascading models: refined requirements inform functional design, which in turn guides logical design and finally the product/technical realization. Governance across lifecycle stages is implicitly supported by standard MBSE practices such as RFLP, OOSEM, SPES and SMArDT, and by tool chains including MathWorks Simulink for simulation and MontiArc for automaton modeling.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Digital Twin development follows MBSE methodologies (SysML, MontiArc, ACME, AADL). Design stages are structured by RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT approaches, and behavior is modeled using SysML v1.7 state machines or MontiArc Automaton. Validation and testing are performed via simulation in MathWorks Simulink and code execution in C++ or Java. Version control and evolution are implicitly handled by the MBSE toolchains, though explicit strategies are not detailed in the document.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly describes the physical system being studied. The text identifies the system as the 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes' and describes its operational domain as 'machining'. It details specific components such as the 'first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor' and includes 'gear transmission' and a 'cycloidal gear'. Therefore, information is present in the document.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text describes specific services provided by the Digital Shadow. The abstract mentions 'visualization in a dashboard' and the 'identification of joint friction'. Section I further notes that the digital shadow enables 'robust, real-time identification of both white- and black-box friction models' and 'integrated visualization within a unified dashboard' to improve the understanding of physical relationships.

The extraction is highly specific and fully supported by the text. It correctly identifies 'ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE)' and 'SysML' as tools for structural modeling. It accurately lists 'MathWorks Simulink' for simulation and mentions general-purpose languages like 'C++ or Java'. It correctly cites Architecture Description Languages such as 'ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs' and correctly identifies 'MQTT' as the communication protocol for data flow.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The document mentions 'real-time, data-driven representations' and 'live measurements' in the context of the digital shadow, but it does not provide specific technical details regarding synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, specific time scales, or temporal granularity needs as requested by the characteristic definition.

The document describes a single digital shadow for a specific asset (the first axis of a robot). It states 'A DS is always created... for a specific Asset' and does not discuss multiple digital twin instances, decentralized architectures, or coordination mechanisms between multiple twins.

While the text details a 'Digital Shadow Reference Model' and internal components, it does not describe system-wide orchestration, resource management, or coordination mechanisms for a Digital Twin system constellation. Quote: 'Fig. 1 presents this refined version of the Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM) as a Unified Modeling Language (UML) class diagram'.

While the document mentions 'Asset Administration Shells (AAS)' and that data is 'streamed via MQTT', it does not describe integration with external systems, interoperability standards with other enterprise systems, or integration patterns with other Digital Twins as defined by the characteristic. The focus is on internal data linking and shadow construction.

block_6_judge.reasoning: The provided text focuses on the technical implementation of a digital shadow for robot motion control, including friction models and data traces (e.g., 'The digital shadow comprises several data traces'). It does not contain any information regarding data governance, ownership policies, privacy regulations, or data sharing agreements. Therefore, the extraction stating 'Not in Document' is accurate.

The source documents discuss the physical system constraints, mentioning that 'The safety cell of the robot restricts the maximum working range,' which is a physical safety feature. However, there is no mention of cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, or digital security measures for the Digital Twin system. Thus, the 'Not in Document' claim is valid regarding the characteristic's specific scope.
